patch.value:
## Story
As an organization running real business workflows on pm (the pm-8ngt vision: companies and their business logic built on pm SDK primitives), I can define principals (roles/teams/agents) and item-level policies — who may create, transition, close, or approve which item types and statuses — and have pm enforce them consistently at the SDK layer, so that the CLI, MCP, and every custom tool built on the SDK apply the same rules.

## Why this is a distinct gap
- pm-f4r1 (shipped) gives per-type allowed status *transitions* — a state machine, but principal-blind: any author may perform any allowed transition.
- Extension sandbox permissions (pm-pl53 lineage) govern what *extensions* may do, not what *authors/agents* may do to items.
- Governance validation (pm-33cw) checks data quality after the fact; there is no policy gate at mutation time.

## Honest constraint (must anchor the ADR)
Git-native storage means anyone with repo write access can bypass the tool entirely. Enforcement is therefore layered, not absolute: (1) advisory/enforcing gate in the SDK mutation path (same knob pattern as governance policy_modes), (2) verification in pm validate + CI so bypasses are detected post-hoc, (3) the real trust boundary stays git (branch protection, required checks). The ADR must state this explicitly rather than promising DB-style ACLs.

## Scope (spike + ADR only, parked)
- Survey: schema/workflows.json extension point for principal rules; author identity model (PM_AUTHOR is self-declared today — policy needs an identity/trust stance); approval-step modeling (Decision items? status gates?).
- Produce an ADR (accept/reject) under pm-m2u1; implementation items only after acceptance.
- Parked at P4 until the v1.0 GA stability contract (pm-agou) fixes the grammar/SDK surface this would extend.

## Duplicate check (2026-07-07 audit #16)
Searched "access control permissions roles", "approval workflow role policy" — matches were extension sandbox permissions and pm-f4r1 transition workflows only; no item covers principal-based item-level policy.
As an organization running business workflows on pm, I can define roles and item-level policies (who may transition/approve what) enforced consistently at the SDK layer

Exploration story (spike + ADR only, parked at P4 like pm-is1a): principal-based item-level policy on top of the shipped per-type transition workflows — layered enforcement (SDK gate, validate/CI verification, git as the real trust boundary), honest about git-native bypass limits. Prerequisite thinking for the pm-8ngt companies-on-pm-SDK vision.

Spike surveys identity/trust stance for PM_AUTHOR, principal rule extension point in schema/workflows, and approval-step modeling; produces an accept/reject ADR under pm-m2u1 that explicitly states the layered-enforcement constraint; no implementation before ADR acceptance and pm-agou v1.0 GA contract

author: maintainer-agent
message: Parked until v1.0 GA stability contract fixes the surface this extends

author: codex-root
message: Connect business policy exploration to SDK-first architecture, universal exemplars, concurrency, security, and horizon governance

value.author: codex-root
value.text: Relationship rationale: role and item policy is an SDK-layer governance capability with universal-domain, concurrency, security, and GA-contract implications. The links preserve the explicit no-implementation-before-ADR constraint.
